chapter: Chapter: Growing up as Boys and Girls (Social and Political Life-II)

questions.question: What does the study of Samoan islands in the 1920s suggest about gender roles?

options.A: Gender roles are the same in every society.
options.B: Children in Samoa were forced to go to school at age five.
options.C: Gender roles are not fixed and can vary significantly across different societies.
options.D: Only boys were allowed to look after younger siblings.

questions.explanation: The Samoan study shows that unlike many other societies, their children had a different upbringing where domestic and outdoor tasks were shared differently across ages.

questions.question: In the 1960s Madhya Pradesh school system, why did girls walk in groups to and from school?

options.A: To play games on the street.
options.B: Because they carried heavy firewood together.
options.C: Due to a fear of being teased or attacked while in the public space.
options.D: To practice marching for school events.

questions.explanation: For girls, the street was not a place to loiter but a passage to be crossed quickly and safely in groups to avoid harassment.

questions.question: How do toys given to children act as a 'silent message' about their future?

options.A: They help children choose their favorite colors.
options.B: They tell children that they will have different roles and futures as men and women.
options.C: They are only meant for entertainment and have no deeper meaning.
options.D: They teach children how to manufacture plastic goods.

questions.explanation: Societies use toys like dolls for girls and cars for boys to subtly reinforce traditional gender expectations for adulthood.

questions.question: Why is the work done by women within the home often described as 'invisible'?

options.A: Because they work only at night.
options.B: Because it is not recognized as actual work and is assumed to come naturally to women.
options.C: Because women do not want anyone to see them working.
options.D: Because the work is very small and takes no time.

questions.explanation: Society tends to devalue housework, viewing it as a natural duty rather than a strenuous labor that deserves recognition.

questions.question: What was the main purpose of Jaspreet going on 'strike' in the story 'My mother does not work'?

options.A: To get a salary from her husband.
options.B: To show the family that the house cannot function without her constant labor.
options.C: To take a vacation in another city.
options.D: To encourage her children to stop going to school.

questions.explanation: The strike highlighted that her 'non-work' was actually a series of endless tasks that kept the household running.
questions.source_section: Story: My Mother does not work

questions.question: According to the data from Haryana and Tamil Nadu mentioned in the book, what is the 'Total hours' spent by women compared to men?

options.A: Women work fewer hours because they stay at home.
options.B: Men work more because they earn money.
options.C: Women spend significantly more hours working (paid + unpaid) than men.
options.D: Both spend exactly the same amount of time.

questions.explanation: Statistical data shows that when housework is added to paid work, women’s total workload far exceeds that of men.

questions.question: Domestic workers like Melani often face 'disrespect' because:

options.A: They do not know how to clean properly.
options.B: Their work is not valued and is considered low-status by their employers.
options.C: They are always late to work.
options.D: They earn more than their employers.

questions.explanation: Low wages and poor treatment of domestic workers stem from the social perception that housework is 'easy' or 'unimportant'.

questions.question: Which of these is a physically demanding task performed by women in rural areas as mentioned in the text?

options.A: Driving tractors.
options.B: Fetching water and carrying heavy headloads of firewood.
options.C: Selling vegetables in the city market.
options.D: Working in local government offices.

questions.explanation: The text notes that many household tasks, especially in rural settings, require heavy physical labor like carrying water over long distances.

questions.question: The 'Double Burden' refers to which group of people?

options.A: Children who have to study two languages.
options.B: Working women who handle both professional jobs and all household responsibilities.
options.C: Men who work two different jobs in a factory.
options.D: Employers who have to pay many domestic workers.

questions.explanation: It describes the dual pressure on women to contribute financially and also perform the bulk of domestic duties.

questions.question: How does the government try to promote equality as mentioned in the chapter?

options.A: By telling men to stay at home.
options.B: By setting up Anganwadis and making laws for crèche facilities in offices.
options.C: By giving free toys to everyone.
options.D: By banning housework for everyone.

questions.explanation: The government intervenes through policy to reduce the burden of childcare on women, enabling them to work and girls to attend school.

questions.question: In the Samoan islands, once a boy was about nine years old, he learned:

options.A: Cooking and cleaning only.
options.B: Outdoor jobs like fishing and planting coconuts.
options.C: How to drive a car.
options.D: To read and write in school.

questions.explanation: After age nine, boys were given more 'adult' outdoor responsibilities, while girls continued to have more freedom until they were older.

questions.question: What is the 'valuation' problem discussed in the chapter regarding housework?

options.A: Housework is too expensive to perform.
options.B: People think housework is very entertaining.
options.C: Societal bias leads to the belief that housework is not real 'work' and therefore has no value.
options.D: Men are better at valuing housework than women.

questions.explanation: The chapter argues that the 'devaluing' of housework is a major source of inequality between genders.

questions.question: The 'provision of crèches' helps in which of the following ways?

options.A: It makes the office look more beautiful.
options.B: It allows women to take up employment and helps more girls attend school.
options.C: It reduces the salary of the workers.
options.D: It encourages children to stay away from their parents.

questions.explanation: By providing a safe place for children, the burden of 'care-giving' is shared, allowing women more opportunities.

questions.question: Why does the author use the story of Melani?

options.A: To show how easy it is to find a job in Delhi.
options.B: To highlight the long hours and hard work involved in domestic service.
options.C: To explain how to cook delicious food.
options.D: To show that domestic workers have a lot of free time.

questions.explanation: Melani's story illustrates the physical toll and the lack of respect domestic workers often experience.

questions.question: Which of the following tasks is categorized under 'care-giving'?

options.A: Washing the family car.
options.B: Looking after sick family members.
options.C: Mowing the lawn.
options.D: Repairing a broken fan.

questions.explanation: Care-giving specifically refers to the emotional and physical support given to children, the elderly, and the infirm.

questions.question: The term 'Strenuous' is used in the chapter to describe housework. What does it mean?

options.A: Very boring and slow.
options.B: Very easy and light.
options.C: Physically and mentally demanding/tough.
options.D: Done only on weekends.

questions.explanation: The chapter corrects the misconception that housework is 'light' work by highlighting the physical effort required.

questions.question: In the 1960s Madhya Pradesh example, how did the boys' school differ from the girls' school?

options.A: The boys' school had no playground.
options.B: The girls' school had a courtyard for privacy, while the boys' playground was an open space attached to the school.
options.C: The boys' school was much smaller.
options.D: The girls' school was in a different city.

questions.explanation: The architecture reflected the societal view that girls needed protection/seclusion, while boys could be in open, public spaces.

questions.question: What is the 'central' reason why inequality between men and women exists according to the chapter?

options.A: Because women are physically weaker.
options.B: Because of social systems that value the work and roles of men more than women.
options.C: Because women do not want to work outside.
options.D: Because there are no laws for equality.

questions.explanation: Inequality is a social construct where the roles traditionally assigned to women are devalued by society.

questions.question: What happened to the dinner in the 'My mother does not work' story during the strike?

options.A: The father cooked a great meal.
options.B: The children cooked for everyone.
options.C: The father struggled and could not manage the household tasks like Jaspreet.
options.D: They ordered food from a hotel and were happy.

questions.explanation: The story shows that the family members who took her work for granted were unable to handle even a fraction of her daily chores.
questions.source_section: Story: My Mother does not work

questions.question: When girls in Samoa reached their teenage years, they:

options.A: Were forced to stay at home forever.
options.B: Had much more freedom and could go on fishing trips.
options.C: Had to stop working.
options.D: Went to colleges in other countries.

questions.explanation: In Samoan society, once girls reached age 14 or so, they enjoyed significant freedom and participated in many outdoor activities.

questions.question: The time-use survey in India shows that on average, women spend ______ hours a week on unpaid housework.

options.C: Between 23 and 30 (varying by state).
options.D: Zero

questions.explanation: The table in the book shows that women in states like Haryana and Tamil Nadu spend a massive amount of time on unpaid work compared to men.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for the low wages of domestic workers?

options.A: The work does not have much value in society.
options.B: Most domestic workers are women.
options.C: The work is considered to be very professional and high-tech.
options.D: Housework is not seen as 'real' work.

questions.explanation: Because society views housework as unskilled and 'natural', it is paid very poorly.

questions.question: What is 'Anganwadi'?

options.A: A type of agricultural land.
options.B: A government-run child care centre in villages.
options.C: A special court for women.
options.D: A factory for making clothes.

questions.explanation: Anganwadis are part of the government's effort to provide childcare and support for families in rural areas.

questions.question: The term 'Devalued' means:

options.A: To increase the price of something.
options.B: When someone is not given due recognition for a task or job they have done.
options.C: To buy something at a discount.
options.D: To work very fast.

questions.explanation: In the chapter, it refers to how society fails to recognize the importance and effort of housework.

questions.question: Gender equality is not just an individual issue; it is a ______ issue.

options.A: Medical
options.B: Social and Political
options.C: Sports
options.D: Climate

questions.explanation: Because inequality is built into the social structure, it requires political action and government laws to fix.
